title: Final letters
subtitle: Ь Ъ Ы Э Ю Я and Ё

hook.text: You've learned 26 of 33 letters. The final seven include two that don't make any sound at all — they only change the sound of the letter next to them. The remaining five are vowels that complete the Russian vowel system. After this lesson, you know the full Cyrillic alphabet.

why.text: The soft sign (Ь) and hard sign (Ъ) are unique to Slavic languages — they control whether consonants are soft or hard. Understanding them is essential for correct pronunciation. The remaining vowels Ы Э Ю Я Ё complete the vowel system and appear in many high-frequency words.

cards.title: Ь — the soft sign
cards.body: Ь has no sound of its own. It makes the preceding consonant 'soft' — meaning your tongue moves slightly upward toward the palate, adding a faint 'y' quality to the consonant. In transliteration it's shown as an apostrophe. Compare мат (mat — mat) with мать (mat' — mother): the final Т is softened by Ь.

examples.meaning: soft sign — softens the preceding consonant

cards.title: Ъ — the hard sign
cards.body: Ъ also has no sound. It's a separator — it appears between a prefix and a root to keep them distinct, preventing the prefix consonant from softening before Е, Ё, Ю, Я. In modern Russian it's rare. You'll see it mainly in formal or compound words. Don't stress over it — just know it exists.

examples.meaning: hard sign — separates prefix from root

cards.title: Ы Э — two distinct vowels
cards.body: Ы is a back vowel with no English equivalent. Say 'ee' and then push your tongue back and lower it slightly — that's Ы. It's often described as the sound you make when punched in the stomach. Э is a plain 'EH' — like the 'e' in 'bed', but without the 'y' element that Е carries.

examples.meaning: back vowel — deeper than 'ee', no English equivalent

cards.title: Ю Я Ё — the iotated vowels
cards.body: These three vowels each start with a 'y' sound. Ю = YOO (like 'you'), Я = YAH (like 'yar'), Ё = YO (like 'yo-yo'). After a consonant, they soften it and drop most of the 'y'. Ё always carries the stress — so if you see Ё, that syllable is stressed. Note: Ё is often printed without its dots in informal Russian text, becoming Е — a frequent source of confusion.

examples.meaning: YO — always stressed, often printed without dots

questions.explanation: Ь = soft sign. It has no sound — it only signals that the consonant before it should be softened (palatalised).

questions.prompt: Ё always signals what about the syllable it's in?
questions.explanation: Ё always carries the stress. If you see Ё in a word, that syllable is stressed — every time.
яблоко (yabloko) = apple. Я at word start sounds like 'ya'.

cards.title: Soft consonants — why it matters
cards.body: When a consonant is soft (followed by Ь or by the iotated vowels Е Ё И Ю Я), it changes its sound slightly — the middle of the tongue rises toward the hard palate. This is a feature of Russian you can't skip. Мать (mother) and мат (obscenity/mat) differ only by the soft sign. Getting Ь right prevents embarrassing errors.

examples.meaning: mother (soft Т — the Ь makes all the difference)

cards.title: Ё without its dots
cards.body: In most printed Russian — newspapers, websites, everyday text — Ё is written as Е (without the two dots). This means you often can't tell from the text whether a word has Ё or Е. Dictionaries and textbooks restore the dots. When learning a new word, check a dictionary to confirm if the Е is actually Ё.

examples.meaning: 'all (people)' vs 'everything' — different words, printed the same way without dots

questions.prompt: What does the soft sign Ь do?
options: Separates two parts of a word
questions.explanation: Ь has no sound — it palatalises (softens) the consonant before it.
мать (mat') = mother. М=m, А=a, Т=t (softened by Ь).

questions.prompt: When Ё appears in a word, what do you know about that syllable?
questions.explanation: Ё always marks the stressed syllable. Use it as a built-in stress indicator.

rules: Ь (soft sign) — no sound, softens the preceding consonant
Ъ (hard sign) — no sound, separates prefix from root; rare in modern Russian
Ы = back 'ee' vowel (no English equivalent); Э = plain 'eh' (like 'e' in 'bed')
Ю = YOO, Я = YAH, Ё = YO — all iotated vowels that soften preceding consonants
Ё always carries stress; in informal text its dots are often omitted, making it look like Е
summary.nextLesson: Next: the full 33-letter alphabet in one reading consolidation lesson — you'll read real Russian words and sentences using everything you've learned.

questions.prompt: Ё always signals that its syllable is:

options: A back vowel with no direct English equivalent
questions.answer: A back vowel with no direct English equivalent

questions.prompt: In everyday Russian text, Ё is often printed as:
